(often abbreviated as **MFA Boston** or **MFA**) is an [art museum](/wiki/Art_museum "Art museum") in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), Massachusetts. It is the [20th-largest](/wiki/List_of_largest_art_museums "List of largest art museums") art museum in the world, measured by public gallery area. It contains 8,161 paintings and more than 450,000 works of art, making it one of the most comprehensive collections in the Americas. With more than 1.2 million visitors a year,[[2]](#cite_note-2) it is the [83rd-most-visited](/wiki/List_of_most-visited_art_museums "List of most-visited art museums") art museum in the world as of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston&action=edit).

The **Museum of Fine Arts** (often abbreviated as **MFA Boston** or **MFA**) is an [art museum](/wiki/Art_museum "Art museum") in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), Massachusetts. It is the [20th-largest](/wiki/List_of_largest_art_museums "List of largest art museums") art museum in the world, measured by public gallery area. It contains 8,161 paintings and more than 450,000 works of art, making it one of the most comprehensive collections in the Americas. With more than 1.2 million visitors a year,[[2]](#cite_note-2) it is the [83rd-most-visited](/wiki/List_of_most-visited_art_museums "List of most-visited art museums") art museum in the world as of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston&action=edit).

Founded in 1870 in [Copley Square](/wiki/Copley_Square "Copley Square"), the museum moved to its current [Fenway](/wiki/Fenway%E2%80%93Kenmore "Fenway–Kenmore") location in 1909. It is affiliated with the [School of the Museum of Fine Arts at Tufts](/wiki/School_of_the_Museum_of_Fine_Arts_at_Tufts "School of the Museum of Fine Arts at Tufts").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1870–1907

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1870–1907")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/MuseumOfFineArtsBoston_CopleySquare_19thc.jpg/250px-MuseumOfFineArtsBoston_CopleySquare_19thc.jpg)](/wiki/File:MuseumOfFineArtsBoston_CopleySquare_19thc.jpg)

The original Museum of Fine Arts building in Copley Square

The Museum of Fine Arts was founded in 1870 and was initially located on the top floor of the [Boston Athenæum](/wiki/Boston_Athen%C3%A6um "Boston Athenæum"). Most of its initial collection came from the Athenæum's Art Gallery.[[3]](#cite_note-bostonaia-3) In 1876, the museum moved to a highly ornamented brick [Gothic Revival](/wiki/Gothic_Revival_architecture "Gothic Revival architecture") building designed by [John Hubbard Sturgis](/wiki/John_Hubbard_Sturgis "John Hubbard Sturgis") and [Charles Brigham](/wiki/Charles_Brigham "Charles Brigham"), noted for its massed architectural [terracotta](/wiki/Terracotta "Terracotta"). It was located in [Copley Square](/wiki/Copley_Square "Copley Square") at Dartmouth and St. James Streets.[[3]](#cite_note-bostonaia-3) It was built almost entirely of brick and terracotta, which was imported from England, with some stone about its base.[[4]](#cite_note-4) After the MFA moved out in 1909, this original building was demolished, and the Copley Plaza Hotel (now the [Fairmont Copley Plaza](/wiki/Fairmont_Copley_Plaza "Fairmont Copley Plaza")) replaced it in 1912.[[5]](#cite_note-5) During the early years of the museum, [Charles Greely Loring](/wiki/Charles_Greely_Loring_(general) "Charles Greely Loring (general)"), a former [Union Army](/wiki/Union_Army "Union Army") general, served as its first director, leading from 1876 to early 1902 when he resigned for health reasons.[[6]](#cite_note-mfa-6): 7

### 1907–1999

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1907–1999")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Museum_of_Fine_Arts%2C_Boston%2C_Mass_%28NYPL_b12647398-74302%29.tiff/lossy-page1-250px-Museum_of_Fine_Arts%2C_Boston%2C_Mass_%28NYPL_b12647398-74302%29.tiff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston,_Mass_(NYPL_b12647398-74302).tiff)

New MFA building in the Fenway, c. 1913–1918

In 1907, plans were laid to build a new home for the museum on [Huntington Avenue](/wiki/Huntington_Avenue "Huntington Avenue") in Boston's [Fenway–Kenmore](/wiki/Fenway%E2%80%93Kenmore "Fenway–Kenmore") neighborhood, near the recently opened [Isabella Stewart Gardner Museum](/wiki/Isabella_Stewart_Gardner_Museum "Isabella Stewart Gardner Museum"). Museum trustees hired architect [Guy Lowell](/wiki/Guy_Lowell "Guy Lowell") to create a design for a museum that could be built in stages, as funding was obtained for each phase. Two years later, the first section of Lowell's [neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") design was completed. It featured a 500-foot (150 m) façade of [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite") and a grand [rotunda](/wiki/Rotunda_(architecture) "Rotunda (architecture)"). The museum moved to its new location in 1909.[[7]](#cite_note-MFA-Arch-7)

The second phase of construction built a wing along [The Fens](/wiki/Back_Bay_Fens "Back Bay Fens") to house painting galleries. It was funded entirely by Maria Antoinette Evans Hunt, the wife of wealthy [business magnate](/wiki/Business_magnate "Business magnate") Robert Dawson Evans, and opened in 1915. From 1916 through 1925, the noted artist [John Singer Sargent](/wiki/John_Singer_Sargent "John Singer Sargent") painted the [frescoes](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco") that adorn the rotunda and the associated [colonnades](/wiki/Colonnade "Colonnade"), using a young Black model, [Thomas McKeller](/wiki/Thomas_McKeller "Thomas McKeller"), as his inspiration for Apollo and the other Greek gods depicted in these murals.[[8]](#cite_note-:1-8)[[7]](#cite_note-MFA-Arch-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Rose_Garden_and_Museum_of_Fine_Arts%2C_Fenway_-_DPLA_-_c8bf98a3b5e7b22bb6fc10431bb8661b.jpg/250px-Rose_Garden_and_Museum_of_Fine_Arts%2C_Fenway_-_DPLA_-_c8bf98a3b5e7b22bb6fc10431bb8661b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rose_Garden_and_Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Fenway_-_DPLA_-_c8bf98a3b5e7b22bb6fc10431bb8661b.jpg)

Rose Garden and Museum of Fine Arts, Fenway, June 14, 1934.

The Decorative Arts Wing was built in 1928, and expanded in 1968. An addition designed by [Hugh Stubbins](/wiki/Hugh_Stubbins "Hugh Stubbins") and Associates was built in 1966–1970, and another expansion by [The Architects Collaborative](/wiki/The_Architects_Collaborative "The Architects Collaborative") opened in 1976. The West Wing, now the Linde Family Wing for Contemporary Art, was designed by [I. M. Pei](/wiki/I._M._Pei "I. M. Pei") and opened in 1981.[[9]](#cite_note-9) This wing now houses the museum's cafe, restaurant, meeting rooms, classrooms, and a giftshop/bookstore, as well as large exhibition spaces.[[7]](#cite_note-MFA-Arch-7) In 1978, the Asiatic Wing was closed because the lack of climate control was jeopardizing the collection. A new control system was contingent upon the completion of the West Wing, and the Asiatic Wing reopened in 1982.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The Tenshin-En Japanese Garden designed by Kinsaku Nakane opened in 1988, and the Norma Jean Calderwood Garden Court and Terrace opened in 1997.[[7]](#cite_note-MFA-Arch-7)[[3]](#cite_note-bostonaia-3)

### 2000–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2000–present")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/MFA_lunch_area_jeh.jpg/250px-MFA_lunch_area_jeh.jpg)](/wiki/File:MFA_lunch_area_jeh.jpg)

The Shapiro Courtyard, which houses [Dale Chihuly](/wiki/Dale_Chihuly "Dale Chihuly")'s *[Lime Green Icicle Tower](/wiki/Lime_Green_Icicle_Tower "Lime Green Icicle Tower")* (right), is used to host large banquets and other events.

In the mid-2000s, the museum launched a major effort to renovate and expand its facilities. In a seven-year fundraising campaign between 2001 and 2008 for a new wing, the [endowment](/wiki/Financial_endowment "Financial endowment"), and operating expenses, the museum managed to receive over $500 million, in addition to acquiring over $160 million worth of art.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 2007, the MFA announced its purchase of a nearby building then occupied by the [Forsyth Institute](/wiki/Forsyth_Institute "Forsyth Institute"), a dental and craniofacial research organization located at 140 [Fenway](/wiki/Fenway_(parkway) "Fenway (parkway)"). The original [Beaux Arts](/wiki/Beaux_Arts_architecture "Beaux Arts architecture") building dates from around 1910, and was later expanded with a Brutalist annex building.[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] The entire property comprised approximately 107,000 square feet (9,900 m2) on 1.6 acres (0.65 ha) of land, located across the street from the main MFA building.[[12]](#cite_note-HuntNews2007-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Artdaily-13) As of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston&action=edit), the building is leased to nearby [Northeastern University](/wiki/Northeastern_University "Northeastern University").[[14]](#cite_note-SmithStJohn-14)

During the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"), the museum's annual budget was trimmed by $1.5 million. The museum increased revenues by organizing traveling exhibitions, which included a loan exhibition sent to the for-profit [Bellagio](/wiki/Bellagio_(resort) "Bellagio (resort)") in Las Vegas in exchange for $1 million. In 2011, [Moody's Investors Service](/wiki/Moody%27s_Investors_Service "Moody's Investors Service") calculated that the museum had over $180 million in outstanding debt. However, the agency cited growing attendance, a large endowment, and positive cash flow as reasons to believe that the museum's finances would become stable in the near future. In 2011, the museum put eight paintings by Monet, Renoir, Pissarro, Sisley, Gauguin, and others on sale at [Sotheby's](/wiki/Sotheby%27s "Sotheby's"), bringing in a total of $21.6 million, to pay for *[Man at His Bath](/wiki/Man_at_His_Bath "Man at His Bath")* by [Gustave Caillebotte](/wiki/Gustave_Caillebotte "Gustave Caillebotte") at a cost reported to be more than $15 million.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/2010_MFA_Boston_Fenway.jpg/250px-2010_MFA_Boston_Fenway.jpg)](/wiki/File:2010_MFA_Boston_Fenway.jpg)

The new Art of the Americas wing is integrated with the neoclassical facade of the main building

A renovation included the new Art of the Americas Wing, featuring artwork from North, South, and Central America. In 2006, the groundbreaking ceremonies took place. The new wing and adjoining Ruth and Carl J. Shapiro Family Courtyard (a bright, cavernous interior space) were designed in a restrained, contemporary style by the London-based architectural firm [Foster and Partners](/wiki/Foster_and_Partners "Foster and Partners"), under the directorship of Thomas T. Difraia and [Childs Bertman Tseckares Architects (CBT)](/w/index.php?title=Childs_Bertman_Tseckares_Inc.&action=edit&redlink=1 "Childs Bertman Tseckares Inc. (page does not exist)"). The landscape architecture firm [Gustafson Guthrie Nichol](/wiki/Kathryn_Gustafson#Firms "Kathryn Gustafson") redesigned the Huntington Avenue and Fenway entrances, gardens, access roads, and interior courtyards.

The wing opened on November 20, 2010, with free admission to the public. Mayor [Thomas Menino](/wiki/Thomas_Menino "Thomas Menino") declared it "Museum of Fine Arts Day", and more than 13,500 visitors attended the opening. The 12,000-square-foot (1,100 m2) glass-enclosed courtyard now features a 42.5-foot (13.0 m) high glass sculpture, titled the *[Lime Green Icicle Tower](/wiki/Lime_Green_Icicle_Tower "Lime Green Icicle Tower")*, by [Dale Chihuly](/wiki/Dale_Chihuly "Dale Chihuly").[[16]](#cite_note-lime-16) In 2014, the Art of the Americas Wing was recognized for its high architectural achievement by the award of the [Harleston Parker Medal](/wiki/Harleston_Parker_Medal "Harleston Parker Medal"), by the [Boston Society of Architects](/wiki/Boston_Society_of_Architects "Boston Society of Architects").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Japanese_gate_and_garden_at_Boston_Museum_of_Fine_Arts_-_August_15%2C_2015.jpg/250px-Japanese_gate_and_garden_at_Boston_Museum_of_Fine_Arts_-_August_15%2C_2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:Japanese_gate_and_garden_at_Boston_Museum_of_Fine_Arts_-_August_15,_2015.jpg)

In 2015, the museum renovated *Tenshin-en*, its [Japanese garden](/wiki/Japanese_garden "Japanese garden").

In 2015, the museum renovated its outdoors [Japanese garden](/wiki/Japanese_garden "Japanese garden"), Tenshin-en. The garden, which originally opened in 1988, had been designed by Japanese professor Kinsaku Nakane. The garden's *kabukimon*-style entrance gate was built by Chris Hall of Massachusetts, using traditional [Japanese carpentry](/wiki/Japanese_carpentry "Japanese carpentry") techniques.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

As a result of the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), the museum was closed from March 12, 2020, through September 25, 2020.[[19]](#cite_note-19) To recover from the drop in attendance caused by the pandemic, a new logo and [branding](/wiki/Branding_(promotional) "Branding (promotional)") campaign were announced in September 2022, along with renewed community outreach efforts. These changes were announced in tandem with the opening of the traveling exhibition of an official portrait of former US president [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") by [Kehinde Wiley](/wiki/Kehinde_Wiley "Kehinde Wiley") and the accompanying portrait of [Michelle Obama](/wiki/Michelle_Obama "Michelle Obama") by [Amy Sherald](/wiki/Amy_Sherald "Amy Sherald"), both on loan from the [National Portrait Gallery](/wiki/National_Portrait_Gallery_(United_States) "National Portrait Gallery (United States)") in Washington DC.[[20]](#cite_note-Shea-20)

In November 2020 a significant number of MFA employees voted to [unionize](/wiki/Unionize "Unionize") due to a long history of unaddressed issues related to workplace conditions and compensation inequities.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The workers unionized with the local chapter of the [United Auto Workers](/wiki/United_Auto_Workers "United Auto Workers"). After over 96% of the union agreed in a vote, MFA staff went on a strike for the first time on November 17, 2021. Union representatives cited unresponsive engagement from MFA management over multiple issues including stagnant wages, job security, and workplace diversity, as the reason for the strike.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The union pointed out that employee wages had been frozen for two years, and that management had so far only offered a 1.75% percent raise over the course of four years. Union representatives contrasted this with MFA director Matthew Teitelbaum's salary which, at nearly US$1 million, was almost 19 times larger than the average MFA worker.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Collection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Collection")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Bunworth_Harp.jpg/250px-Bunworth_Harp.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bunworth_Harp.jpg)

The "[Bunworth harp](/wiki/Charles_Bunworth "Charles Bunworth")", a large [cláirseach](/wiki/Celtic_harp "Celtic harp") made in 1734, is part of the collection of musical instruments

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Appeal_to_the_Great_Spirit%2C_Boston_MFA_-_IMG_3401.JPG/250px-Appeal_to_the_Great_Spirit%2C_Boston_MFA_-_IMG_3401.JPG)](/wiki/File:Appeal_to_the_Great_Spirit,_Boston_MFA_-_IMG_3401.JPG)

[Cyrus Dallin](/wiki/Cyrus_Dallin "Cyrus Dallin")'s *[Appeal to the Great Spirit](/wiki/Appeal_to_the_Great_Spirit "Appeal to the Great Spirit")* (1908) stands outside the museum's main entrance facing Huntington Avenue.

The Museum of Fine Arts possesses materials from a wide variety of art movements and cultures. The museum also maintains a large online database with information on over 346,000 items from its collection, accompanied with digitized images. Online search is freely available through the Internet.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The museum also maintains the Conservation and Art Materials Encyclopedia Online, (CAMEO) a [database](/wiki/Database "Database") that "compiles, defines, and disseminates technical information on the distinct collection of terms, materials, and techniques used in the fields of art conservation and historic preservation".[[25]](#cite_note-CAMEO:About-25) CAMEO uses [MediaWiki](/wiki/MediaWiki "MediaWiki").[[26]](#cite_note-CAMEO_MediaWiki_API_help-26)

### Highlights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Highlights")]

Some highlights of the collection include:

* [Ancient Egyptian](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") [artifacts](/wiki/Artifact_(archaeology) "Artifact (archaeology)") including sculptures, [sarcophagi](/wiki/Sarcophagus "Sarcophagus"), and jewelry dating back to approximately 6500 BCE to 600 CE, which were primarily obtained through excavations conducted by [George A. Reisner](/wiki/George_Andrew_Reisner "George Andrew Reisner") in Egypt and Sudan between 1905 and 1942.[[27]](#cite_note-mfa.org-27)
* The [Nubian](/wiki/Nubians "Nubians") arts, including [Kerma](/wiki/Kerma "Kerma") pottery, colossal royal statues of [Napatan](/wiki/Napata "Napata") kings, jewels, and imports from Greece and Rome, are represented in the collection of Nubian art.[[27]](#cite_note-mfa.org-27)
* [Dutch Golden Age painting](/wiki/Dutch_Golden_Age_painting "Dutch Golden Age painting"), including 113 works given in 2017 by collectors Rose-Marie and Eijk van Otterloo and Susan and Matthew Weatherbie.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The gift includes works from 76 artists, as well as the [Haverkamp-Begemann](/wiki/Egbert_Haverkamp-Begemann "Egbert Haverkamp-Begemann") Library, a collection of more than 20,000 books, donated by the van Otterloos. The donors also established in 2022 a scholarly institute at the museum, the Center for Netherlandish Art, which houses the library and many of the paintings.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)
* [French Impressionist](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism") and [Post-Impressionist](/wiki/Post-Impressionist "Post-Impressionist") works by artists such as [Paul Gauguin](/wiki/Paul_Gauguin "Paul Gauguin"), [Édouard Manet](/wiki/%C3%89douard_Manet "Édouard Manet"), [Pierre-Auguste Renoir](/wiki/Pierre-Auguste_Renoir "Pierre-Auguste Renoir"), [Edgar Degas](/wiki/Edgar_Degas "Edgar Degas"), [Claude Monet](/wiki/Claude_Monet "Claude Monet"), [Camille Pissarro](/wiki/Camille_Pissarro "Camille Pissarro"), [Vincent van Gogh](/wiki/Vincent_van_Gogh "Vincent van Gogh"), and [Paul Cézanne](/wiki/Paul_C%C3%A9zanne "Paul Cézanne")
* 18th- and 19th-century American art, including many works by [John Singleton Copley](/wiki/John_Singleton_Copley "John Singleton Copley"), [Winslow Homer](/wiki/Winslow_Homer "Winslow Homer"), [John Singer Sargent](/wiki/John_Singer_Sargent "John Singer Sargent"), and [Gilbert Stuart](/wiki/Gilbert_Stuart "Gilbert Stuart")
* [Cyrus Dallin](/wiki/Cyrus_Dallin "Cyrus Dallin")'s 1908 statue, *[Appeal to the Great Spirit](/wiki/Appeal_to_the_Great_Spirit "Appeal to the Great Spirit")*, is prominently exhibited on the lawn at the museum's entrance.
* The Chinese collections feature a selection of imperial [ceramics](/wiki/Ceramic "Ceramic"), ancient bronzes, monumental [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhist_art "Buddhist art") sculptures, and 20th-century and contemporary artwork, spanning nearly every era of Chinese history. The collection of Chinese paintings is particularly noteworthy, with numerous masterpieces from the [Song](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") and [Yuan](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty") dynasties.[[31]](#cite_note-Art_of_Asia-31)
* The largest collection of Japanese artworks under one roof in the world outside Japan[[31]](#cite_note-Art_of_Asia-31)
* The [Hartley Collection](/wiki/Harold_Hartley_(businessman) "Harold Hartley (businessman)") of almost 10,000 British illustrated books, prints and drawings from the late 19th century
* The Rothschild Collection, including over 130 objects from the Austrian branch of the [Rothschild family](/wiki/Rothschild_family "Rothschild family"). Donated by [Bettina Burr](/wiki/Bettina_Burr "Bettina Burr") and other heirs[[32]](#cite_note-32)
* The Rockefeller collection of Native American work[[33]](#cite_note-33)
* The Linde Family Wing for Contemporary Art includes works by [Kathy Butterly](/wiki/Kathy_Butterly "Kathy Butterly"), [Mona Hatoum](/wiki/Mona_Hatoum "Mona Hatoum"), [Jenny Holzer](/wiki/Jenny_Holzer "Jenny Holzer"), [Karen LaMonte](/wiki/Karen_LaMonte "Karen LaMonte"), [Ken Price](/wiki/Kenneth_Price "Kenneth Price"), [Martin Puryear](/wiki/Martin_Puryear "Martin Puryear"), [Doris Salcedo](/wiki/Doris_Salcedo "Doris Salcedo"), and [Andy Warhol](/wiki/Andy_Warhol "Andy Warhol").[[34]](#cite_note-34)
* A collection of over 1,100 historical music instruments, with selected items displayed in a dedicated music room, with occasional talks, live demonstrations, and concerts.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### Japanese art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Japanese art")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Katsushika_Hokusai%2C_published_by_Nishimuraya_Yohachi_%28Eijud%C5%8D%29_-_Fine_Wind%2C_Clear_Weather_%28Gaif%C5%AB_kaisei%29%2C_also_known_as_Red_Fuji%2C_from_the_series_Thirty-six_Views_o..._-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Katsushika_Hokusai,_published_by_Nishimuraya_Yohachi_(Eijud%C5%8D)_-_Fine_Wind,_Clear_Weather_(Gaif%C5%AB_kaisei),_also_known_as_Red_Fuji,_from_the_series_Thirty-six_Views_o..._-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*Red Fuji*, from the series *[Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji](/wiki/Thirty-six_Views_of_Mount_Fuji "Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji")* by [Hokusai](/wiki/Hokusai "Hokusai"), c. 1830–1832

The collection of Japanese art at the Museum of Fine Arts is the largest in the world outside of Japan.[[31]](#cite_note-Art_of_Asia-31) Anne Nishimura Morse, the William and Helen Pounds Senior Curator of Japanese Art, oversees 100,000 total items[[36]](#cite_note-(CULCON)-2012-36) that include 4,000 Japanese paintings, 5,000 ceramic pieces, and over 30,000 [ukiyo-e](/wiki/Ukiyo-e "Ukiyo-e") prints.[[37]](#cite_note-Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

The base of this collection was assembled in the late 19th century through the efforts of four men, [Ernest Fenollosa](/wiki/Ernest_Fenollosa "Ernest Fenollosa"), [Kakuzo Okakura](/wiki/Okakura_Kakuz%C5%8D "Okakura Kakuzō"), [William Sturgis Bigelow](/wiki/William_Sturgis_Bigelow "William Sturgis Bigelow"), and [Edward Sylvester Morse](/wiki/Edward_S._Morse "Edward S. Morse"), each of whom had spent time in Japan and admired Japanese art.[[36]](#cite_note-(CULCON)-2012-36)[[39]](#cite_note-39) Their combined donations account for up to 75 percent of the current collection.[[36]](#cite_note-(CULCON)-2012-36) In 1890, the Museum of Fine Arts became the first museum in the United States to establish a collection and appoint a curator specifically for Japanese art.[[37]](#cite_note-Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston-37)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Another part of this collection is a number of [Buddhist statues](/wiki/Buddhist_art_in_Japan "Buddhist art in Japan"). In the later [Meiji era](/wiki/Meiji_(era) "Meiji (era)") of Japan, around the turn of the 20th century, government policy deemphasizing [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") in favor of [Shintoism](/wiki/State_Shinto "State Shinto") and financial pressures on temples resulted in a number of Buddhist statues being sold to private collectors. Some of these statutes came into the collection of the Museum of Fine Arts.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Billman-2020-42) Today, these statues are the subject of preservation and restoration efforts, which have been at times viewable by the public in special exhibits.[[42]](#cite_note-Billman-2020-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43) In recent years, the museum has also collected a number of works by contemporary Japanese artists. In 2011, they acquired *Zetsu* *no. 8* (絶), the largest work in ceramicist [Jun Nishida](/wiki/Jun_Nishida "Jun Nishida")'s *Zetsu (絶)* series.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

Also important for this collection is the exhibition of its items in Japan. From 1999 to 2018, regular exchange of items was conducted between the Museum of Fine Arts and its sister museum, the now-closed [Nagoya/Boston Museum of Fine Arts](/wiki/Nagoya/Boston_Museum_of_Fine_Arts "Nagoya/Boston Museum of Fine Arts").[[37]](#cite_note-Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston-37)[[45]](#cite_note-45) In 2012, the traveling exhibition *Japanese Masterpieces from the Museum of Fine Arts, Boston* visited the Japanese cities of Tokyo, Nagoya, Osaka and Fukuoka, and was well received.[[36]](#cite_note-(CULCON)-2012-36)[[37]](#cite_note-Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston-37)[[46]](#cite_note-46)

In 2023 the museum held an exhibition entitled "Hokusai: Inspiration and Influence" celebrating the origins, works, and cultural impact of Japanese artist [Hokusai](/wiki/Hokusai "Hokusai").[[47]](#cite_note-47)

### Chinese art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Chinese art")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Nine-Dragons1.jpg/250px-Nine-Dragons1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nine-Dragons1.jpg)

Nine Dragons (detail), Chen Rong, Chinese, Southern Song dynasty, dated 1244. Handscroll, ink and color on paper, 46.8 × 1495.6 cm. Museum of Fine Arts, Boston, Francis Gardner Curtis Fund, 17.1697.

The Chinese art collection at the Museum of Fine Arts is one of the finest outside East Asia, with particularly strong holdings in paintings, ceramics, and Buddhist sculpture.[[48]](#cite_note-48) The collection includes objects from nearly every era of Chinese history, from Neolithic tomb artifacts to contemporary works.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

The museum's Chinese paintings collection is internationally renowned. Highlights include the *Thirteen Emperors* scroll from the Tang dynasty (618–907), considered the oldest court painting in existence, as well as *[Court Ladies Preparing Newly Woven Silk](/wiki/Court_Ladies_Preparing_Newly_Woven_Silk "Court Ladies Preparing Newly Woven Silk")* (early 12th century, attributed to Emperor Huizong) and [*Nine Dragons*](/wiki/Nine_Dragons_(painting) "Nine Dragons (painting)") (first half of the 13th century, [Chen Rong](/wiki/Chen_Rong_(painter) "Chen Rong (painter)")).[[50]](#cite_note-50) The ceramics collection features important imperial stonewares and porcelain, including a rare [Ru ware](/wiki/Ru_ware "Ru ware") dish from the Northern Song dynasty, one of fewer than one hundred such pieces still extant in the world.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

In 2018, the museum received the largest and most significant gift of Chinese paintings and calligraphy in its history: the Wan-go H. C. Weng Collection. Encompassing 130 paintings, 31 works of calligraphy, 18 ink rubbings, and 4 textiles, the gift spans 13 centuries and five imperial dynasties, with particularly rich representation of art from the Ming (1368–1644) and Qing (1644–1911) eras.[[52]](#cite_note-52) The core of this collection was assembled in the 19th century by [Weng Tonghe](/wiki/Weng_Tonghe "Weng Tonghe") (1830–1904), a preeminent scholar who served as tutor to two of the last emperors of the Qing dynasty.

### Restitution claims

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Restitution claims")]

In April 2024, the museum donated several 14th-century Buddhist [Śarīra](/wiki/%C5%9Aar%C4%ABra "Śarīra") relics that were in its collection since 1939 to the [Jogye Order](/wiki/Jogye_Order "Jogye Order") as part of an agreement to repatriate artifacts taken from [Korea](/wiki/Korea "Korea") during the [Japanese Occupation](/wiki/Korea_under_Japanese_rule "Korea under Japanese rule"). The relics will be transferred to the Heoam Temple in [Yangju](/wiki/Yangju "Yangju"), South Korea, where they are believed to have originated.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

In November 2025, the museum returned two of its [Benin Bronzes](/wiki/Benin_Bronzes "Benin Bronzes") to Nigeria.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

## Libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Libraries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Horticultural_Hall_Massachusetts_Horticultural_Society_Boston.jpg/250px-Horticultural_Hall_Massachusetts_Horticultural_Society_Boston.jpg)](/wiki/File:Horticultural_Hall_Massachusetts_Horticultural_Society_Boston.jpg)

The main library was formerly located nearby on Massachusetts Avenue at [Horticultural Hall](/wiki/Horticultural_Hall_(Boston) "Horticultural Hall (Boston)").

The libraries at the Museum of Fine Arts consist of a main library, eight curatorial departmental libraries, and the Center for Netherlandish Art Library.[[55]](#cite_note-mfalib-55) Collectively they hold over 450,000 items, including 60,000 art auction catalogs, and 150,000 periodicals and ephemera.[[55]](#cite_note-mfalib-55) The main branch, the [William Morris Hunt](/wiki/William_Morris_Hunt "William Morris Hunt") Memorial Library, is named after the noted American artist. In 2021, the main library moved after 18 years at [Horticultural Hall](/wiki/Horticultural_Hall_(Boston) "Horticultural Hall (Boston)"), two stops away on the [MBTA Green Line](/wiki/MBTA_Green_Line "MBTA Green Line").[[56]](#cite_note-56) The new entrance for the library is on the first floor of the museum, near Sharf Information Center, in front of the Nina Saunders Suite. About one-quarter of the collection was planned to be housed on the third floor of the museum, along with the book conservation facilities, with the remainder stored off-site.[[57]](#cite_note-LibNewsletter-57) The main library had been open to the public, and the catalog could be searched online through the Fenway Libraries Online (FLO).[[58]](#cite_note-libvisitus-58) It is open to researchers for two three-hour sessions per week, but only by appointment requested two weeks in advance, and subject to approval.[[55]](#cite_note-mfalib-55)[[58]](#cite_note-libvisitus-58) Exhibitions organized by the library staff in coordination with the [School of the Museum of Fine Arts at Tufts](/wiki/School_of_the_Museum_of_Fine_Arts_at_Tufts "School of the Museum of Fine Arts at Tufts") have been debuted two to three times per year.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

## Community relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Community relations")]

The MFA also has a longstanding initiative within the Community Arts program called the [Community Artist Initiative Artist Project](https://www.mfa.org/programs/community-programs/community-arts-initiative), where the museum invites a Lead Artist to spend nine months creating works with youth from twelve after-school community organizations in the Boston area. The Artist and the children create a collaborative work of art inspired by the Museum's encyclopedic collection, and the completed project is exhibited in the Edward H. Linde Gallery (168) in the Linde Family Wing for Contemporary Art at the MFA.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

The MFA offers accessibility accommodations for visitors who may be visually, audibly, or physically impaired.[[61]](#cite_note-Access-61) Special programming and tours are available for blind, [ASL](/wiki/ASL "ASL")-fluent, cognitively-impaired, [autistic](/wiki/Autism "Autism"), and medically assisted guests.[[62]](#cite_note-62) In the spring of 2019 it installed new signage for its [restrooms](/wiki/Public_toilet "Public toilet"), in an effort towards "restroom accessibility for people of all genders and abilities."[[63]](#cite_note-VisTips-63)

The MFA publicly apologized in May 2019 after African-American and mixed-race 12- and 13-year-old visitors were allegedly targeted by employees and told "No food, no drink, and no watermelon", which is considered a [racial slur](/wiki/Watermelon_stereotype "Watermelon stereotype") in the US.[[64]](#cite_note-Apology-64) A museum spokesperson said that the warning was actually "no water bottles", but conceded that there was no way of definitively proving what was actually said. Regardless, all museum staff dealing with school groups were to be retrained in interactions with their guests. The MFA also concluded that two of its members had been deliberately racist, and permanently banned them from visiting its grounds.[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67)

In 2019 the MFA debuted its newly renamed "Indigenous Peoples' Day" (formerly [Columbus Day](/wiki/Columbus_Day "Columbus Day")) celebrations, with a focus on Native American art and culture.[[68]](#cite_note-IPday-68) The events included special displays related to [Cyrus Dallin](/wiki/Cyrus_Dallin "Cyrus Dallin")'s 1908 *[Appeal to the Great Spirit](/wiki/Appeal_to_the_Great_Spirit "Appeal to the Great Spirit")*, a popular and sometimes controversial sculpture of a Native American warrior located in front of the Huntington Avenue main entrance since 1912. Community comments and feedback concerning the monumental artwork were solicited and displayed.[[68]](#cite_note-IPday-68) Earlier, in March 2019, the MFA had held a special public symposium to discuss the historical background and present-day significance of the sculpture.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

In 2020 the MFA had planned to offer 11 annual Community Celebrations, featuring free admission for all visitors, and special events such as dance performances, music, tours, craft demonstrations, and hands-on art making. This series included day-long [Martin Luther King Jr. Day](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr._Day "Martin Luther King Jr. Day"), [Lunar New Year](/wiki/Lunar_New_Year "Lunar New Year"), [Memorial Day](/wiki/Memorial_Day "Memorial Day"), [Highland Street Foundation](/w/index.php?title=Highland_Street_Foundation&action=edit&redlink=1 "Highland Street Foundation (page does not exist)") Free Fun Friday, and [Indigenous Peoples' Day](/wiki/Indigenous_Peoples%27_Day_(United_States) "Indigenous Peoples' Day (United States)") celebrations. In addition, on Wednesday evenings, which were already free from 4pm to 10pm, special celebrations of [Nowruz](/wiki/Nowruz "Nowruz"), [Juneteenth](/wiki/Juneteenth "Juneteenth"), [Latinx Heritage Night](/wiki/National_Hispanic_Heritage_Month "National Hispanic Heritage Month"), ASL Night, [Diwali](/wiki/Diwali "Diwali"), and [Hanukkah](/wiki/Hanukkah "Hanukkah") were featured.[[70]](#cite_note-CommCel-70)

To commemorate its 150th anniversary, the MFA offered a free one-year family membership to anyone who attended one of its special Community Celebrations or MFA Late Nite programs during 2020. This "First Year Free Membership" program was available to anyone who had not previously been a member of the museum.[[71]](#cite_note-FYFM-71) The 150th year exhibitions included major shows and events featuring art by women and minority artists.[[72]](#cite_note-mfa150-72)[[73]](#cite_note-Close-73)[[74]](#cite_note-Chew-74)

## Special exhibitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Special exhibitions")]

The Museum of Fine Arts, Boston, hosts special exhibitions consisting of items borrowed from other museums. These limited time events extend for several months and there are typically three exhibitions taking place concurrently since at least 1996. The collection topics of the special exhibits are divided into 13 categories: Africa and Oceania; Americas; Ancient Egypt, [Nubia](/wiki/Nubia "Nubia") and the near East; Ancient Greece and Rome; Asia; [Contemporary Art](/wiki/Contemporary_art "Contemporary art"); Europe; Jewelry; Judaica; Musical Instruments; Photography; Prints and Drawings; and Textile and Fashion Arts.[[75]](#cite_note-75) Most special exhibitions take place at the following galleries: Edward and Nancy Roberts Family Gallery, Linde Family Wing for Contemporary Art, Ann and Graham Gund Gallery, Edward H. Linde Gallery. The exhibitions are usually open to public with a variety of ticket prices. Members of MFA, Boston can also enjoy free admission to any special exhibitions.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

### *[Philip Guston](/wiki/Philip_Guston "Philip Guston") Now*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Philip Guston Now")]

*Philip Guston Now* is an exhibition organized by the Museum of Fine Arts, Boston; [the National Gallery of Art, Washington](/wiki/National_Gallery_of_Art "National Gallery of Art"); the [Museum of Fine Arts, Houston](/wiki/Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Houston "Museum of Fine Arts, Houston"); and [Tate Modern](/wiki/Tate_Modern "Tate Modern"), London. This show is also considered as America's most controversial art exhibition, as of May 2022. Its controversies are mainly due to much of his work addressing and confronting topics such as [white supremacy](/wiki/White_supremacy "White supremacy"), racism, [anti-Semitism](/wiki/Antisemitism "Antisemitism"), and violence[[77]](#cite_note-77) It was originally planned to open in June 2020, but due to nationwide protests for [racial equality](/wiki/Racial_equality "Racial equality"), it was postponed to open at May. 1, 2022.[[78]](#cite_note-78) The museums were aware of its controversies, so they opened a comment area on their official website for the public to share their thoughts with the museum.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

## Bulletin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Bulletin")]

A bulletin appeared under various titles from 1903 to 1983:[[83]](#cite_note-83)

* 1903–1925: *Museum of Fine Arts Bulletin*
* 1926–1965: *Bulletin of the Museum of Fine Arts*
* 1966–1977: *Boston Museum Bulletin*
* 1978–1980: *MFA Bulletin*
* 1981–1983: *M Bulletin (Museum of Fine Arts, Boston)*
